comments.body: Isn't it just the same as my explanation to the previous question, but the other way around? In this case, when you scroll, you set tx, then you set ty, then you set scale. Setting scale updates the slider value (via the binding), which invokes the listener on the slider's value property, which then sets tx and ty (overriding the values you set in the scroll event handler) assuming you are pivoting on the center.
Closing as duplicate since a couple of minor edits to the original address this question.

title: How binding the slider value property to a scale property makes the pivot calculation go wrong in a zoom and pan application
body:
Introdution

In a previous post, I asked about why the zoom-by-slider doesn't work as expected when I bind the slider bidirectionally to the scale, to ensure that the slider updates during wheel zooming.

In a comment, James_D suggested that the slider might be updating the scale property before the listener on the slider's value property is invoked. He was correct, which is why the newScale - oldScale always equals 0, causing the listener to return and exit each time.

The fixes and the issue

To fix the issue, I used slider.valueProperty().addListener((_, oldV, newV) instead of slider.valueProperty().addListener((_, _, newV) to access the previous value of the property before it changes, due to the binding.

Although the issue vanished when zooming with the slider, it now appears when zooming with the wheel, shifting to the same positions mentioned earlier.

I also noticed that the slider's minimum value isn't updating when the window resizes. To fix this, the CENTER_FIT_SCALE is recalculated whenever the viewport's layoutBoundsProperty changes. This enables setting the slider's minimum to Math.min(viewport.getWidth() / image.getWidth(), viewport.getHeight() / image.getHeight()), representing the scale factor that ensures the image fits within its container while maintaining its aspect ratio.

To verify that both zoom-by-slider and zoom-by-wheel are using the correct oldScale and newScale values, I perform successive zoom actions using one method, then switch to the other, printing the new and old scale each time. My aim is to ensure the slider sets the new scale and the wheel calculation are accurate, and when resuming the old scale is becoming the new scale. This showed that the values are well set and calculated, and they resume correctly when switching.

After extensive checks and debugging, the problem appears to be related to the Pivot. In the wheel method, here's how the tx and ty are set:

 tx.set(tx.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getX());
 ty.set(ty.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getY());

And this is how they are set in the slider:

 Point2D pivotOnImage = imageView.sceneToLocal(viewport.localToScene(viewport.getWidth() / 2, viewport.getHeight() / 2));
            tx.set(tx.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getX());
            ty.set(ty.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getY());

Now, debugging the pivot values won't be simple because I need to position the mouse at the exact same point to compare the calculated pivot values in both methods: when the slider value property is bound and when it is not. However, the differences are quite large, so it's clear the calculated pivot is the source of the issue, as you can verify with simple prints.

The other thing that confirms that the pivot is the cause is when removing pivotOnImage.getX(), pivotOnImage.getY(), from the formula setting the tx and ty in the slider, the issue shifts from the wheel method back to the slider!!!!

The updated code

import javafx.application.Application;
import javafx.beans.property.DoubleProperty;
import javafx.beans.property.SimpleDoubleProperty;
import javafx.geometry.Point2D;
import javafx.scene.Scene;
import javafx.scene.image.Image;
import javafx.scene.image.ImageView;
import javafx.scene.input.MouseButton;
import javafx.scene.input.ScrollEvent;
import javafx.scene.layout.BorderPane;
import javafx.scene.layout.Pane;
import javafx.scene.paint.Color;
import javafx.scene.shape.Rectangle;
import javafx.scene.transform.Affine;
import javafx.stage.Stage;
import javafx.scene.control.Slider;
import javafx.util.StringConverter;

import java.util.Objects;

public class ZoomAndShiftImage extends Application {
    private final double MAX_SCALE = 8.0;
    private static double CENTER_FIT_SCALE;

    @Override
    public void start(Stage stage) {
        Image image = new Image(Objects.requireNonNull(Objects.requireNonNull(getClass().getResourceAsStream("/playground/bird.jpg"))));
        ImageView imageView = new ImageView(image);
        imageView.setPreserveRatio(true);
        imageView.setSmooth(true);

        Affine affine = new Affine();
        imageView.getTransforms().add(affine);

        Pane viewport = new Pane(imageView);
        Rectangle clip = new Rectangle();

        clip.widthProperty().bind(viewport.widthProperty());
        clip.heightProperty().bind(viewport.heightProperty());

        viewport.setClip(clip);
        viewport.setStyle("-fx-background-color: #1d2026;");

        final Delta drag = new Delta();

        DoubleProperty scale = new SimpleDoubleProperty(1.0);
        DoubleProperty tx = new SimpleDoubleProperty(0);
        DoubleProperty ty = new SimpleDoubleProperty(0);

        Runnable applyTransform = () -> affine.setToTransform(scale.get(), 0, tx.get(), 0, scale.get(), ty.get());

        scale.addListener((_, _, _) -> applyTransform.run());
        tx.addListener((_, _, _) -> applyTransform.run());
        ty.addListener((_, _, _) -> applyTransform.run());
        applyTransform.run();

        Slider slider = getSlider();
        slider.setMax(MAX_SCALE);
        slider.valueProperty().bindBidirectional(scale);

        viewport.layoutBoundsProperty().addListener((_, _, _) -> {
            CENTER_FIT_SCALE = Math.min(viewport.getWidth() / image.getWidth(), viewport.getHeight() / image.getHeight());
            slider.setMin(CENTER_FIT_SCALE);

            centerFit(imageView, viewport, scale, tx, ty);
        });

        viewport.setOnMousePressed(e -> {
            if (e.getButton() == MouseButton.PRIMARY) {
                // Save the current coordinates of the click
                drag.x = e.getX();
                drag.y = e.getY();
            }
        });

        viewport.setOnMouseDragged(e -> {
            double dx = e.getX() - drag.x;
            double dy = e.getY() - drag.y;

            drag.x = e.getX();
            drag.y = e.getY();

            tx.set(tx.get() + dx);
            ty.set(ty.get() + dy);
        });

        viewport.setOnMouseClicked(e -> {
            if (e.getClickCount() == 2 && e.getButton() == MouseButton.PRIMARY) {
                scale.set(1.0);
                tx.set(0.0);
                ty.set(0.0);

                centerFit(imageView, viewport, scale, tx, ty);
            }
        });

        viewport.addEventFilter(ScrollEvent.SCROLL, e -> {
            Point2D pivotOnImage = imageView.sceneToLocal(e.getSceneX(), e.getSceneY());

            if (!imageView.getBoundsInLocal().contains(pivotOnImage))
                return;

            double direction = (e.getDeltaY() > 0) ? 1.1 : (1 / 1.1);
            double oldScale = scale.get();
            double newScale = clamp(oldScale, direction, CENTER_FIT_SCALE);

            System.out.println("wheel: " + oldScale + " | " + newScale + " | " + CENTER_FIT_SCALE);

            if (newScale == 0) {
                centerFit(imageView, viewport, scale, tx, ty);
                return;
            }

            tx.set(tx.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getX());
            ty.set(ty.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getY());

            scale.set(newScale);
            e.consume();
        });

        slider.valueProperty().addListener((_, oldV, newV) -> {
            double oldScale = oldV.doubleValue();
            double newScale = newV.doubleValue();

            if (Math.abs(newScale - oldScale) < 1e-9)
                return;

            if (newV.doubleValue() <= CENTER_FIT_SCALE) {
                centerFit(imageView, viewport, scale, tx, ty);
                scale.set(CENTER_FIT_SCALE);
            }

            System.out.println("slider: " + oldScale + " | " + newScale + " | " + CENTER_FIT_SCALE);

            Point2D pivotOnImage = imageView.sceneToLocal(viewport.localToScene(viewport.getWidth() / 2, viewport.getHeight() / 2));
            tx.set(tx.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getX());
            ty.set(ty.get() + (oldScale - newScale) * pivotOnImage.getY());

            // getting the issue back
            // tx.set(tx.get() + (oldScale - newScale) );
            // ty.set(ty.get() + (oldScale - newScale) );
            // scale.set(newScale);
        });

        BorderPane root = new BorderPane(viewport);
        root.setBottom(slider);

        Scene scene = new Scene(root, 900, 600, Color.BLACK);
        stage.setTitle("Zoom & Pan");
        stage.setScene(scene);
        stage.show();
    }

    private void centerFit(ImageView imageView, Pane viewport, DoubleProperty scale, DoubleProperty tx, DoubleProperty ty) {
        Image image = imageView.getImage();
        if (image == null || image.getWidth() <= 0 || image.getHeight() <= 0)
            return;

        double viewportWidth = viewport.getWidth();
        double viewportHeight = viewport.getHeight();

        if (viewportWidth <= 0 || viewportHeight <= 0)
            return;

        double sx = viewportWidth / image.getWidth();
        double sy = viewportHeight / image.getHeight();
        double fit = Math.min(sx, sy);

        scale.set(fit);

        double imageWidth = image.getWidth();
        double imageHeight = image.getHeight();

        double scaledW = imageWidth * fit;
        double scaledH = imageHeight * fit;

        tx.set((viewportWidth - scaledW) / 2.0);
        ty.set((viewportHeight - scaledH) / 2.0);
    }

    private static double clamp(double oldScale, double direction, double DEFAULT_SCALE) {
        double d = Math.min(oldScale * direction, 8.0);

        if (d >= 2 * DEFAULT_SCALE || direction > 1)
            return d;
        else if (d <= DEFAULT_SCALE)
            return 0;

        return d;
    }

    private static class Delta {
        double x, y;
    }

    private Slider getSlider() {
        Slider slider = new Slider();
        slider.setBlockIncrement((1 / 1.1));
        slider.setMajorTickUnit(1.0);
        slider.setMinorTickCount(9);

        slider.setLabelFormatter(new StringConverter<>() {
            @Override
            public String toString(Double v) {
                if (Math.abs(v - 0.0) < 1e-6) return "0.25x";
                if (Math.abs(v - 0.5) < 1e-6) return "0.5x";
                if (Math.abs(v - 1.0) < 1e-6) return "1x";
                if (Math.abs(v - 2.0) < 1e-6) return "2x";
                if (Math.abs(v - 4.0) < 1e-6) return "4x";
                if (Math.abs(v - 8.0) < 1e-6) return "8x";
                return String.format("%.2fx", v);
            }

            @Override
            public Double fromString(String s) {
                return 1.0;
            }
        });
        return slider;
    }
}

I would like to understand how binding the slider value property to the scale property causes these issues and the pivot calculations to go awry.

PS: While I am writing this post and before publishing it, James_D posted an answer that works but does not use the bind. Thanks, James :)
